nativeRange.habitat: The Red Shiner inhabits silty, sandy, and rocky pools, runs, and occasionally riffles of creeks and small to medium rivers across central North America, and is notably tolerant of siltation, turbidity, reduced water quality, and seasonally intermittent flows.

decorPreferences: smooth rocks and pebble substrate mimicking stream bed
fine-leaved plants or java moss for spawning crevices
driftwood or flat stones as shelter
powerhead or circulation pump to provide moderate current

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality flake, micro-pellets, and frozen or live foods including brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms, and mosquito larvae. Also grazes on soft algae and vegetable matter; spirulina-based foods enhance male breeding coloration. Wild diet consists of aquatic and terrestrial insects and algae. Feed 2–3 small portions daily.

avoidWith: long-finned species (betta, fancy guppies, angels) — fin-nipping risk
very slow or sedentary fish that cannot compete at feeding
tiny dwarf shrimp fry (may be eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Tom Kennedy (1024x683). Vivid nuptial male on black background: iridescent blue-green flank, pink-purple crown, and bright red-orange dorsal/anal/caudal fins — every diagnostic breeding feature crisp and in frame.
Photo by Marine discovery (3072x2304). Breeding male held in hand showing electric-blue flank, olive-green back and orange-red fins; sharp and whole-bodied, hand background the only minor caveat.
USFWS (Sam Stukel), Gavins Point NFH (4800x3052). Clean studio black-background shot, tack-sharp, strong pink-blue-green iridescence; fins less reddened than the recommended pick.

summary: The Red Shiner is a small, active minnow from central North America that is widely considered hardy and thrives in unheated aquariums. Males develop iridescent pink, purple, and blue flanks with vivid red-orange fins during the breeding season, making this an appealing choice for cool-water community tanks.
careNotes: The Red Shiner is a small, active schooling minnow ideally suited to unheated indoor aquariums in the 10–24°C range, making it a strong choice for temperate setups alongside White Cloud Mountain Minnows, zebra danios, and hillstream loaches. A group of at least 6 (ideally 10+) is necessary for natural schooling behaviour and to distribute any mild intraspecific chasing. The tank should be well-filtered and well-oxygenated; moderate water movement via a powerhead or spray bar replicates the flowing-creek conditions the species prefers. Water chemistry requirements are not demanding — neutral to slightly alkaline, moderately hard water (pH 7.0–7.5, dH 10–20) suits the species well. The Red Shiner is notably tolerant of lower temperatures and cooler seasonal fluctuations, and has been kept successfully in outdoor ponds in temperate climates over summer. Avoid housing with bettas, angelfish, or other long-finned fish, as the Red Shiner tends to nip fins. The species was formerly placed in Cyprinidae but is now classified in Leuciscidae. Invasive concern: widely established outside its native Mississippi basin and Gulf drainage range through bait-bucket releases; do not release aquarium fish into natural waterways. Though commonly sold as a feeder fish, it is also kept as a display species.
breedingNotes: Crevice spawner; spawning season runs mid-April through September at temperatures around 22–26°C. Females release multiple egg clutches daily into rock crevices, gaps in decor, or among coarse substrate, averaging 400–600 eggs per clutch across many clutches per season. Young-of-year fish are capable of spawning, accelerating establishment in new environments. Eggs hatch in 3–5 days; fry become free-swimming after approximately one week. Parents do not guard eggs and will eat them — remove adults or provide dense moss cover. Feed fry infusoria or liquid fry food initially, transitioning to baby brine shrimp once free-swimming. Breeding males display spectacular iridescent pink-purple-blue flanks with a red crown and vivid red-orange fins.

Also listed as 'Rainbow Dace'.

fish.breedingNotes: The Red Shiner spawns readily in mature tanks at 22–26°C when provided with rock gaps, coarse substrate, or similar crevice-like structures. It produces multiple clutches per season, with each clutch averaging 400–600 eggs. Eggs typically hatch within 3–5 days, and fry become free-swimming after approximately one week. Parents do not guard eggs and will consume them given the opportunity, so adults should be removed once spawning is complete.
